text: Welcome to this video of a very special left VATS diaphragm placation. I'll just introduce the case.
This patient has a paralysed hemi diaphragm on the left.
The surgeon inserts the first port behind the scapula using a guided technique with a camera placed in the trocar, preparing for the thoracoscopic approach.
The surgeon uses an early entry port with CO₂ emission at the tip, allowing visualization of the lung in front of the port tip as it safely enters the chest cavity.
The surgeon uses a 10mm camera for enhanced visualization during the procedure, with CO₂ insufflation to maintain a clear view of the surgical field.
The CO₂ insufflation is set at eight millimeters of mercury pressure, visibly depressing the diaphragm downward.

Under direct vision, the surgeon places a second five-millimeter port for the five-millimeter graspers. Posteriorly, just a rib space above the pushed-down diaphragm, an eight-millimeter port is placed for the FlexDex needle holder, a six-millimeter device.
The FlexDex needle holder is shown, packaged in a large sterile container. The device includes three wrist supports for the surgeon, and is designed for single-use applications.

The three different sizes, you're meant to put it just distal to your styloid process, just on your wrist, and the soft part goes on the under part of your wrist. And it's very easy to put on. You just put your hand in, and then there's two little buttons that you can click so that the base of the FlexDex needle holder articulates on your wrist.

The surgeon demonstrates the movement of the FlexDex needle holder, showing how hand movements control the tip of the instrument. The needle holder is inserted through the port, ready for use in the procedure.
The surgeon uses a second instrument as a grasper, preparing to perform diaphragm plication under direct vision with the diaphragm visibly pushed down by CO₂ insufflation.
The surgeon assesses the diaphragm, which appears extremely floppy and thin, as the initial step in the diaphragm placation procedure.

The surgeon assesses the diaphragm, which is pushed downward due to the volume of CO₂ insufflation in the chest cavity. The diaphragm appears redundant, and the surgeon evaluates the areas suitable for placing the first placation stitches.
The surgeon prepares to suture using the FlexDex needle holder, which is articulated at the wrist for enhanced maneuverability.

The surgeon demonstrates the articulation of the FlexTex needle holder, which allows for approximately eighty degrees of movement in any direction. The needle holder is used to perform a diaphragm plication with a series of horizontal mattress sutures, starting with the first stitch placed anteriorly.

The diaphragm is visibly draped over the spleen, with an NG tube present in the patient's stomach for decompression. The surgeon employs an overlapping technique, doubling the diaphragm over itself for safety during the procedure.
The surgeon demonstrates the thin, overlapped diaphragm, ensuring there is no risk of catching abdominal contents during the procedure.
The surgeon uses the FlexDex needle holder to perform a series of horizontal mattress sutures on the diaphragm, maintaining a comfortable arm position at ninety degrees.
This is the very first case that I've done with the Flex-Tex needle holder. So I was experimenting with the exact technique.
The surgeon performs additional horizontal mattress sutures to tighten the initial suture, using the FlexDex needle holder for precise manipulation.

The surgeon uses the FlexDex needle holder to throw knots by rotating around the hub of the device, demonstrating the ease of knot tying with the instrument's adjustable head for grasping and pulling.
The surgeon places the first suture using a 2-0 braided non-absorbable suture, demonstrating the knot-tying technique. The process is repeated multiple times, ensuring the suture remains secure.
So I thought I'd carry on with the suturing here. Now, just for comparison, this is one of my robotic cases on the right, and I just wanted to show you really how similar it is.

You can see all the advantages or what you need to do for a diaphragm is to be able to have great ability and flexibility to suture in any direction. And really, other than I'm using my needle holder in my left hand with the Flex-Tex and I'm using right hand With the robot, there is no real difference.

So I really think in terms of the technical differences between this procedure you're seeing now and a robotic diaphragm placation, which I also perform and also have videos on YouTube if you want to see them. The main difference is time. It takes quite a lot of time to set up the robot. Access to the robot. We have limited access. We don't have our own robot. We need an extra port with the robot as well for the assistant to pass in the noodles. It's not very simple to constantly bring in and out the robotic arms. And bigger ports. The robot currently needs larger ports than what you're seeing here today.

So to perform this with the FlexDex really you're going to need a five mil port for the cameras, use a five mil camera, second five mil port and then this eight mil port and perhaps as you know robotics would require one extra port. My SI has a thirteen millimetre camera port, twelve millimetre camera port and eight millimetre trocars and a utility port for the assistant would be twelve mils.
And time is the other thing. It's definitely a longer procedure, robotically, just due to the positioning of the patient and the time taken to dock.

So you're seeing me doing some more horizontal mattress sutures which I'll then knot again to the original previous suture to make a nice, good first stitch. I think the first stitch is absolutely vital in diaphragm placation. It pulls the diaphragm right down into position and also sets up the overlapping nature of the diaphragm on both sides, making it very simple to do further sutures.
The surgeon completes the first key suture using a single pledget to prevent tissue cut-through, preparing the area for further diaphragm plication.
The surgeon quickly switches from using a grasper to scissors during the VATS procedure, demonstrating the faster instrument change compared to robotic surgery.
The surgeon uses the Flex-Tex needle holder to place a second horizontal mattress suture, demonstrating precise needle handling and angle control.

um as well this is quite difficult if you don't use an articulated needle holder and i don't think you need two articulated instruments i don't think the one on the right my vats grasper would benefit at all really from also being articulated i think it's just the needle holder that's important

And there's a lot of very helpful resources on the flexdex.com website. One American surgeon I was watching who has an expert practice in ventral hernias said one other thing that I don't have is three division.

I think it is still very helpful to have three division if you can and there are some bats cameras that have three division and that is something that I did miss compared to robotics and the reason that is is because it's just a little bit more difficult to pick up the needle and and exactly knowing three dimensions where you are so if you do have the ability to do a bat style application with the flex next needle holder and you can get a three-d camera all the better

And then the only other thing that VATS loses out on compared to robotics is having your ability as the surgeon to control the camera. So you might see at some stage on this video that sometimes it's not perfectly centralized, the action, and that is something that having an automated camera or a robotic system is an advantage of.

Here's an image of robotic versus FlexDex knot tie. The reason for showing this really is just to show how similar it is and then finally cutting the suture. I think virtually identical really. With erotics, as you can see there, I use my assistants to cut the suture.

Really the perfect way to do a diaphragm placation I think must be with the FlexDex noodle holder with a three-D camera and if possible with a kind of powered camera holder such as Autolap or there's a new company called Endocontrol that has a voice-activated camera holder. That would be fantastic and then you've really built yourself your own surgeon-powered robot.

What could be better? So back to the diaphragm placation. I like to do diaphragm placations as tight as I possibly can. There's kind of a rumor, or you hear it on videos quite a lot, that people say not too tight. I don't really understand what not too tight is. I suppose not too tight means the suture's tearing through, but if you're not actually seeing them tearing through, then I think the tighter the better, really.

The tighter it is, the further down the diaphragm will be on the post-operative chest x-ray. I used to do it a little bit looser and the post-op chest x-rays were really quite disappointing in how little the diaphragm moved, although they did get some symptomatic benefit. I think the tighter I do them, the better the benefit for patients, although obviously I haven't done a controlled trial in that, but that is my impression, having now done about forty diaphragm placations.

also abandoned pledges i used to very strictly do um ten pledges on both sides um i was very worried about cutting through but uh seeing the strength of these um i no longer think pledges are necessary and i got quite a few plural effusions and i just wondered whether having a large amount of teflon with a paralyzed diaphragm that's not moving might just be causing these pleural effusions.
I still leave the drain in two or three days just because I'm worried about effusions, but I haven't had any effusions since I gave up using teflon pledges and just did long lines of suture.

This is two-oh, and the reason it's two-oh is because I'm using a suture called a B-fifty, B-fifty-two suture, which has quite a small needle on it, and it's small enough to fit down the five millimetre port anteriorly. I haven't yet tracked down a zero suture with a needle this small, although if I did I would use it.

There are some other methods of doing diaphragm placations. One's using the endo-stitch by Convidian and that does have a zero suture. And I think the stronger the better really for the suture. I think there is no advantage to a smaller suture at all.
And just occasionally if you pull very hard this suture, even at two I can snap. But I've had no early failures at all. But a few sutures have snapped a little bit while doing the knotting.
The surgeon performs multiple horizontal mattress sutures, opting for this technique to expedite the suturing process.
The surgeon ties knots using a suture within the surgical field, demonstrating the knot tying technique.

are lots of different methods for doing a placation i wouldn't say that this is the only way although i think multiple sutures is a good idea i have done a redo diaphragm placation where i found a single protein suture that had fractured in the chest from another center and that one suture had failed and then the whole repair had failed so i think multiple sutures is good I also haven't really come to a conclusion on how long these repairs might last. The diaphragm is still paralysed and the large band of muscle above the suture line is still paralysed and the forces from the abdomen are still going to be present on it. So I do worry a little that the diaphragm will continue to stretch in these patients over coming years.

So if the patients are quite young, by that I mean in their twenties or thirties, I tend to put a mesh over the top of the diaphragm just to try and make it last as long as possible. And sometimes I anchor that into ribs from inside by doing a large suture either around the rib or into the perichondrium.

One thing about the FlexTex needle holder is that like any new device or like starting robotics, you need to practice. No surgeon should expect to be able to get it out of the packet and just start using it. It isn't like a robotic arm. It doesn't exactly follow your arm movement because it's half a VATS instrument and half a wristed instrument.

so the wristing follows your wrist identically but the movement in the chest is still as a vats instrument so when your hand goes up the instrument goes down and this requires some practice but luckily there's a lot of resources on the flexdex website and they even have a little low fidelity rig to help you practice and i'm sure everybody's got endoscopic

simulation suites in their hospital or little day fidelity ways of practicing I certainly would recommend doing a little bit of practice before your first case once you've done one or even two you will have spent a good couple of hours operatively practicing, and this will mean that you wouldn't have to practice in the future, but it is something that you should invest a little bit of time just practicing.

So, here we are. Here's the final result. There's a lovely tight diaphragm. This person was delighted with the results of their operation. Their shortness of breath improved immediately. And there's the FlexTex needle holder just saying thank you very much, taking a bow from the audience. And I think it's done a great job. I'm sure you'll agree it's done great. So thank you very much for watching. And I'd love to hear some comments about that and what you think. Thank you very much.
